name: Linux File System Hierarchy
description: Linux directory structure, file system types, and mounting concepts
key_facts: FHS root directories: /etc (configuration), /var (variable data/logs), /home (user dirs), /tmp (temporary)
/bin and /sbin contain essential system binaries; /usr/bin contains user programs
Common file systems: ext4 (default), XFS (high performance), Btrfs (snapshots/checksums)
mount command attaches a file system to a directory; /etc/fstab defines persistent mounts
Inodes store file metadata (permissions, owner, timestamps) but not the filename
common_misconceptions: Deleting files from /tmp is always safe (running processes may have open handles to temp files)
Linux has drive letters like Windows (Linux uses a single root hierarchy with mount points)

name: File Permissions and Ownership
description: Linux file permissions, ownership, special permissions, and ACLs
key_facts: Permission notation: rwx (read=4, write=2, execute=1) for owner, group, others
chmod changes permissions (chmod 755 file); chown changes ownership (chown user:group file)
SUID (4xxx) runs a file as the file owner; SGID (2xxx) runs as the group owner or inherits group on directories
Sticky bit (1xxx) on a directory prevents users from deleting files they do not own (e.g., /tmp)
ACLs (setfacl/getfacl) provide fine-grained permissions beyond the standard owner/group/others model
common_misconceptions: Root is subject to file permissions (root can read/write any file regardless of permissions)
chmod 777 is a quick fix (it creates a serious security vulnerability by granting everyone full access)

name: Process and Service Management
description: Managing processes, services, and system resources in Linux
key_facts: systemd is the default init system on modern Linux; systemctl manages services
ps aux lists all running processes; top/htop show real-time system resource usage
kill sends signals to processes: SIGTERM (15) for graceful shutdown, SIGKILL (9) for forced termination
nice and renice set process priority; lower values mean higher priority (range -20 to 19)
Background processes: command & runs in background; nohup keeps it running after logout
common_misconceptions: kill always terminates a process (kill sends SIGTERM by default, which can be caught and ignored; kill -9 forces termination)
All processes are visible to all users (process visibility depends on kernel configuration and namespaces)

name: Package Management
description: Installing, updating, and managing software packages on different Linux distributions
key_facts: Debian/Ubuntu: apt (apt-get, apt-cache) uses .deb packages; dpkg for local package management
RHEL/Fedora: dnf (replaces yum) uses .rpm packages; rpm for local package management
Repositories are configured in /etc/apt/sources.list (Debian) or /etc/yum.repos.d/ (RHEL)
apt update refreshes package lists; apt upgrade installs available updates
Dependency resolution is handled automatically by apt and dnf
common_misconceptions: apt and yum packages are interchangeable (.deb and .rpm are different formats for different distributions)
apt update installs updates (apt update only refreshes the package list; apt upgrade installs them)

name: User and Group Management
description: Creating and managing user accounts, groups, and authentication in Linux
key_facts: useradd creates users; usermod modifies users; userdel removes users
/etc/passwd stores user account info; /etc/shadow stores hashed passwords
UID 0 is root; UIDs 1-999 are typically system accounts; 1000+ are regular users
sudo grants temporary elevated privileges; /etc/sudoers configured with visudo
PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) provides a flexible authentication framework
common_misconceptions: Users should log in as root for administrative tasks (sudo provides controlled elevated access without exposing the root password)
/etc/passwd contains passwords (passwords are stored as hashes in /etc/shadow)

name: Linux Firewalls and Security Hardening
description: Firewall configuration, SELinux/AppArmor, and system hardening practices
key_facts: iptables and nftables filter network traffic using chains: INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD
firewalld provides a dynamic frontend with zones and services for easier management
SELinux uses mandatory access control with contexts (enforcing, permissive, disabled modes)
AppArmor uses path-based mandatory access control profiles
SSH hardening: disable root login, use key-based authentication, change default port, use fail2ban
common_misconceptions: Disabling SELinux fixes permission problems (it removes a security layer; the correct approach is to configure proper contexts)
Firewalls are unnecessary on internal servers (defense in depth requires firewalls on all systems)

name: Shell Scripting Basics
description: Bash scripting fundamentals including variables, loops, conditionals, and common utilities
key_facts: Shebang line (#!/bin/bash) specifies the interpreter for the script
Variables: VAR=value (no spaces); accessed with $VAR or ${VAR}
Control structures: if/then/fi, for/do/done, while/do/done, case/esac
Exit codes: 0 means success; non-zero means failure; $? holds the last exit code
Common text processing: grep (search), sed (stream edit), awk (pattern processing), cut (extract columns)
common_misconceptions: Shell scripts are always slower than compiled programs (for system administration tasks, shell scripts are often the most efficient tool)
Spaces around the = in variable assignment are fine (VAR = value causes an error; it must be VAR=value)

name: Containers and Orchestration
description: Container concepts, Docker basics, and container orchestration with Kubernetes
key_facts: Containers share the host OS kernel, making them lighter than VMs
Docker images are built from Dockerfiles and stored in registries
docker run creates and starts a container; docker ps lists running containers
Kubernetes orchestrates container deployment, scaling, and management across clusters
Podman is a daemonless alternative to Docker that can run rootless containers
common_misconceptions: Containers are small VMs (containers share the host kernel and do not run a separate OS)
Docker and Kubernetes compete (Docker creates containers; Kubernetes orchestrates them)

name: Log Management and Analysis
description: System logging, journal management, and log analysis for troubleshooting
key_facts: journalctl queries the systemd journal; journalctl -u sshd shows logs for a specific service
Traditional log files are stored in /var/log: syslog, messages, auth.log, kern.log
rsyslog and syslog-ng collect and forward log messages; configuration in /etc/rsyslog.conf
Log rotation (logrotate) manages log file sizes with compression and deletion policies
dmesg shows kernel ring buffer messages, useful for hardware and boot-time troubleshooting
common_misconceptions: All logs are in /var/log (applications may log to custom locations; systemd journal stores binary logs)
journald replaces rsyslog completely (both can coexist and complement each other)

name: Storage and Network Troubleshooting
description: Diagnosing disk, LVM, RAID, and network connectivity issues in Linux
key_facts: df -h shows file system disk space usage; du -sh shows directory sizes
LVM (Logical Volume Manager) allows dynamic resizing of partitions: PV, VG, LV layers
fsck checks and repairs file system errors (must be run on unmounted file systems)
ss (replaces netstat) shows socket statistics; ss -tlnp lists listening TCP ports
ip command replaces ifconfig: ip addr show, ip route show, ip link set
common_misconceptions: Running fsck on a mounted file system is safe (it can corrupt data; unmount first or boot into rescue mode)
ifconfig is still the standard tool (ip command is the modern replacement on all current distributions)
